Mary Blair  ​  ​ (m. 1923; div. 1930)​   Margaret Canby  ​  ​ (m. 1930; died 1932)​   [Mary McCarthy](/wiki/Mary_McCarthy_(author) "Mary McCarthy (author)")  ​  ​ (m. 1938; div. 1946)​   Elena Thornton  ​ (m. 1946)​

(May 8, 1895 – June 12, 1972) was an American writer, [literary critic](/wiki/Literary_critic "Literary critic"), and journalist. He is widely regarded as one of the most important literary critics of the 20th century. Wilson began his career as a journalist, writing for publications such as *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(American_magazine_1913%E2%80%931936) "Vanity Fair (American magazine 1913–1936)")* and *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")*. He helped to edit *[The New Republic](/wiki/The_New_Republic "The New Republic")*, served as chief book critic for *The New Yorker*, and was a frequent contributor to *[The New York Review of Books](/wiki/The_New_York_Review_of_Books "The New York Review of Books")*.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American writer and literary critic (1895–1972)

For other people named Edmund Wilson, see [Edmund Wilson (disambiguation)](/wiki/Edmund_Wilson_(disambiguation) "Edmund Wilson (disambiguation)").

| Edmund Wilson | |
| --- | --- |
| [Wilson in 1936](/wiki/File:Edmund_Wilson.jpg "Wilson in 1936") Wilson in 1936 | |

| Occupation | * [Literary critic](/wiki/Literary_critic "Literary critic") * essayist * editor * journalist * writer |

| Notable works | * *[Axel's Castle](/wiki/Axel%27s_Castle "Axel's Castle")* (1931) * *[To the Finland Station](/wiki/To_the_Finland_Station "To the Finland Station")* (1940) * *[Patriotic Gore](/wiki/Patriotic_Gore "Patriotic Gore")* (1962) |
| Notable awards | * [Edward MacDowell Medal](/wiki/Edward_MacDowell_Medal "Edward MacDowell Medal") (1964) * [National Medal for Literature](/wiki/National_Medal_for_Literature "National Medal for Literature") (1966) |
| Spouses | Mary Blair  ​  ​ (m. 1923; div. 1930)​   Margaret Canby  ​  ​ (m. 1930; died 1932)​   [Mary McCarthy](/wiki/Mary_McCarthy_(author) "Mary McCarthy (author)")  ​  ​ (m. 1938; div. 1946)​   Elena Thornton  ​ (m. 1946)​ |

**Edmund Wilson Jr.** (May 8, 1895 – June 12, 1972) was an American writer, [literary critic](/wiki/Literary_critic "Literary critic"), and journalist. He is widely regarded as one of the most important literary critics of the 20th century. Wilson began his career as a journalist, writing for publications such as *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(American_magazine_1913%E2%80%931936) "Vanity Fair (American magazine 1913–1936)")* and *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")*. He helped to edit *[The New Republic](/wiki/The_New_Republic "The New Republic")*, served as chief book critic for *The New Yorker*, and was a frequent contributor to *[The New York Review of Books](/wiki/The_New_York_Review_of_Books "The New York Review of Books")*.

His notable works include *[Axel's Castle](/wiki/Axel%27s_Castle "Axel's Castle")* (1931), described by [Joyce Carol Oates](/wiki/Joyce_Carol_Oates "Joyce Carol Oates") as "a groundbreaking study of modern literature." Oates writes that Wilson "encroached fearlessly on areas reserved for academic 'experts': early Christianity in *The Dead Sea Scrolls* (1955), native American civilization in *Apologies to the Iroquois* (1960), and the American Civil War in *[Patriotic Gore](/wiki/Patriotic_Gore "Patriotic Gore")* (1962)."[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Wilson also authored a novel, *I Thought of Daisy* (1929) and a collection of short stories, *[Memoirs of Hecate County](/wiki/Memoirs_of_Hecate_County "Memoirs of Hecate County")* (1946). He was a friend of many notable figures, including [F. Scott Fitzgerald](/wiki/F._Scott_Fitzgerald "F. Scott Fitzgerald"), [Ernest Hemingway](/wiki/Ernest_Hemingway "Ernest Hemingway"), [John Dos Passos](/wiki/John_Dos_Passos "John Dos Passos") and [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov"). His dream for a [Library of America](/wiki/Library_of_America "Library of America") series of national classic works came to fruition through the efforts of [Jason Epstein](/wiki/Jason_Epstein "Jason Epstein") after Wilson's death. He was a two-time winner of the [National Book Award](/wiki/National_Book_Award "National Book Award") and received the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom") in 1964. He died in 1972 at age 77.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edmund_Wilson&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Wilson was born in [Red Bank, New Jersey](/wiki/Red_Bank,_New_Jersey "Red Bank, New Jersey"). His parents were [Edmund Wilson Sr.](/wiki/Edmund_Wilson_Sr. "Edmund Wilson Sr."), a lawyer who served as [New Jersey Attorney General](/wiki/New_Jersey_Attorney_General "New Jersey Attorney General"), and Helen Mather (née Kimball). Wilson attended [The Hill School](/wiki/The_Hill_School "The Hill School"), a college preparatory boarding school in [Pottstown, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pottstown,_Pennsylvania "Pottstown, Pennsylvania"), graduating in 1912. At Hill, Wilson served as the editor-in-chief of the school's [literary magazine](/wiki/Literary_magazine "Literary magazine"), *The Record*.

From 1912 to 1916, he was educated at [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"), where his friends included [F. Scott Fitzgerald](/wiki/F._Scott_Fitzgerald "F. Scott Fitzgerald") and [war poet](/wiki/War_poet "War poet") [John Allan Wyeth](/wiki/John_Allan_Wyeth_(poet) "John Allan Wyeth (poet)"). Wilson began his professional writing career as a reporter for the *[New York Sun](/wiki/New_York_Sun_(historical) "New York Sun (historical)")*, and served in the army with Base Hospital 36 from Detroit, Michigan, and later as a translator during the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). His family's summer home at [Talcottville, New York](/wiki/Talcottville,_New_York "Talcottville, New York"), known as [Edmund Wilson House](/wiki/Edmund_Wilson_House "Edmund Wilson House"), was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 1973.[[2]](#cite_note-nris-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edmund_Wilson&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Wilson was the managing editor of *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(American_magazine_1913-1936) "Vanity Fair (American magazine 1913-1936)")* in 1920 and 1921, and later served as associate editor of *[The New Republic](/wiki/The_New_Republic "The New Republic")* and as a book reviewer for *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")* and *[The New York Review of Books](/wiki/The_New_York_Review_of_Books "The New York Review of Books")*. His works influenced novelists [Upton Sinclair](/wiki/Upton_Sinclair "Upton Sinclair"), [John Dos Passos](/wiki/John_Dos_Passos "John Dos Passos"), [Sinclair Lewis](/wiki/Sinclair_Lewis "Sinclair Lewis"), [Floyd Dell](/wiki/Floyd_Dell "Floyd Dell"), and [Theodore Dreiser](/wiki/Theodore_Dreiser "Theodore Dreiser"). He served on the [Dewey Commission](/wiki/Dewey_Commission "Dewey Commission") that set out to fairly evaluate the charges that led to the exile of [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky"). He wrote plays, poems, and novels, but his greatest influence was literary criticism.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

*[Axel's Castle: A Study in the Imaginative Literature of 1870–1930](/wiki/Axel%27s_Castle "Axel's Castle")* (1931) was a sweeping survey of [Symbolism](/wiki/Symbolism_(arts) "Symbolism (arts)"). It covered [Arthur Rimbaud](/wiki/Arthur_Rimbaud "Arthur Rimbaud"), [Auguste Villiers de l'Isle-Adam](/wiki/Auguste_Villiers_de_l%27Isle-Adam "Auguste Villiers de l'Isle-Adam") (author of *[Axël](/wiki/Ax%C3%ABl "Axël")*), [W. B. Yeats](/wiki/W._B._Yeats "W. B. Yeats"), [Paul Valéry](/wiki/Paul_Val%C3%A9ry "Paul Valéry"), [T. S. Eliot](/wiki/T._S._Eliot "T. S. Eliot"), [Marcel Proust](/wiki/Marcel_Proust "Marcel Proust"), [James Joyce](/wiki/James_Joyce "James Joyce"), and [Gertrude Stein](/wiki/Gertrude_Stein "Gertrude Stein").

In 1931, monitoring the [Coal War](/wiki/Coal_Wars "Coal Wars") in [Harlan County](/wiki/Harlan_County,_Kentucky "Harlan County, Kentucky"), with [Mary Heaton Vorse](/wiki/Mary_Heaton_Vorse "Mary Heaton Vorse") and Malcolm Cowley he was run out of Kentucky by nightriders.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1932, Wilson pledged his support to the [Communist Party USA](/wiki/Communist_Party_USA "Communist Party USA")'s candidate for president, [William Z. Foster](/wiki/William_Z._Foster "William Z. Foster"), signing a manifesto in support of CPUSA policies; however, Wilson did not identify personally as a communist.[[7]](#cite_note-Newyorker-7) In his book *[To the Finland Station](/wiki/To_the_Finland_Station "To the Finland Station")* (1940), Wilson traced the course of European socialism, from the 1824 discovery by [Jules Michelet](/wiki/Jules_Michelet "Jules Michelet") of the ideas of [Vico](/wiki/Giambattista_Vico "Giambattista Vico") to the 1917 arrival of [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") at the Finland Station of [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg") to lead the [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolshevism "Bolshevism") in the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution").

In an essay on the work of horror writer [H. P. Lovecraft](/wiki/H._P._Lovecraft "H. P. Lovecraft"), "Tales of the Marvellous and the Ridiculous", Wilson condemned Lovecraft's tales as "hackwork".[[8]](#cite_note-8) Wilson is also well known for his heavy criticism of [J. R. R. Tolkien](/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien")'s *[The Lord of the Rings](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings "The Lord of the Rings")*, which he referred to as "juvenile trash", saying "Dr. Tolkien has little skill at narrative and no instinct for literary form."[[9]](#cite_note-9) He had earlier dismissed the work of [W. Somerset Maugham](/wiki/W._Somerset_Maugham "W. Somerset Maugham") in vehement terms (without, as he later boasted, having troubled to read the novels generally regarded as Maugham's finest, *[Of Human Bondage](/wiki/Of_Human_Bondage "Of Human Bondage")*, *[Cakes and Ale](/wiki/Cakes_and_Ale "Cakes and Ale")* and *[The Razor's Edge](/wiki/The_Razor%27s_Edge "The Razor's Edge")*).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In 1964, Wilson was awarded The [Edward MacDowell Medal](/wiki/Edward_MacDowell_Medal "Edward MacDowell Medal") by [The MacDowell Colony](/wiki/The_MacDowell_Colony "The MacDowell Colony") for outstanding contributions to American culture.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Wilson lobbied for the creation of a series of classic U.S. literature similar to France's *[Bibliothèque de la Pléiade](/wiki/Biblioth%C3%A8que_de_la_Pl%C3%A9iade "Bibliothèque de la Pléiade")*. In 1982, ten years after his death, [The Library of America](/wiki/The_Library_of_America "The Library of America") series was launched.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Wilson's writing was included in the Library of America in two volumes published in 2007.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Peers and relationships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edmund_Wilson&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Peers and relationships")]

Wilson's critical works helped foster public appreciation for several novelists: [Ernest Hemingway](/wiki/Ernest_Hemingway "Ernest Hemingway"), [John Dos Passos](/wiki/John_Dos_Passos "John Dos Passos"), [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner"), [F. Scott Fitzgerald](/wiki/F._Scott_Fitzgerald "F. Scott Fitzgerald"), and [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov"). He was instrumental in establishing the modern evaluation of the works of [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") and [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling").[[14]](#cite_note-14) Wilson was a friend of the novelist and playwright [Susan Glaspell](/wiki/Susan_Glaspell "Susan Glaspell") as well as the philosopher [Isaiah Berlin](/wiki/Isaiah_Berlin "Isaiah Berlin").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

He attended Princeton with Fitzgerald, a year-and-a-half his junior. In 1936 in [the "Crack-Up" essays](/wiki/The_Crack-Up#Essays "The Crack-Up"), Fitzgerald referred to Wilson as his "intellectual conscience ... [f]or twenty years".[[16]](#cite_note-16) After Fitzgerald's early death (at the age of 44) from a heart attack in December 1940, Wilson edited two books by Fitzgerald (*[The Last Tycoon](/wiki/The_Last_Tycoon "The Last Tycoon")* and *[The Crack-Up](/wiki/The_Crack-Up "The Crack-Up")*) for posthumous publication, donating his editorial services to help Fitzgerald's family. Wilson was also a friend of Nabokov, with whom he corresponded extensively and whose writing he introduced to Western audiences. However, their friendship was marred by Wilson's cool reaction to Nabokov's *[Lolita](/wiki/Lolita "Lolita")* and irretrievably damaged by Wilson's public criticism of what he considered Nabokov's eccentric translation of [Pushkin](/wiki/Alexander_Pushkin "Alexander Pushkin")'s *[Eugene Onegin](/wiki/Eugene_Onegin "Eugene Onegin").*

Wilson had multiple marriages and affairs.

* His first wife was Mary Blair, who had been in [Eugene O'Neill](/wiki/Eugene_O%27Neill "Eugene O'Neill")'s theatrical company. Their daughter, Rosalind Baker Wilson, was born on September 19, 1923.
* His second wife was Margaret Canby. After her death in a freak accident two years after their marriage, Wilson wrote a long eulogy to her and said later that he felt guilt over having neglected her. Wilson, despondent over Canby's death, moved to a rundown townhouse at [314 East 53rd Street](/wiki/314_East_53rd_Street "314 East 53rd Street") in Manhattan for several years.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* From 1938 to 1946, he was married to [Mary McCarthy](/wiki/Mary_McCarthy_(author) "Mary McCarthy (author)"), who like Wilson was well known as a literary critic. She enormously admired Wilson's breadth and depth of intellect, and they collaborated on numerous works. In an article in *The New Yorker*, [Louis Menand](/wiki/Louis_Menand "Louis Menand") wrote, "The marriage to McCarthy was a mistake that neither side wanted to be first to admit. When they fought, he would retreat into his study and lock the door; she would set piles of paper on fire and try to push them under it."[[19]](#cite_note-19) This marriage resulted in the birth of their son, Reuel, on December 25, 1938.[[20]](#cite_note-Theroux-20)
* His fourth wife was Elena Mumm Thornton (previously married to [James Worth Thornton](/wiki/James_Worth_Thornton "James Worth Thornton")).[[20]](#cite_note-Theroux-20) Their daughter, [Helen Miranda Wilson](/wiki/Helen_Miranda_Wilson "Helen Miranda Wilson"), was born February 19, 1948.

He wrote many letters to [Anaïs Nin](/wiki/Ana%C3%AFs_Nin "Anaïs Nin"), criticizing her for her surrealistic style, because it was opposed to the [realism](/wiki/Literary_realism "Literary realism") that was then deemed correct writing, and he ended by asking for her hand — "I would love to be married to you, and I would teach you to write" — which she took as an insult.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Except for a brief falling-out following the publication of *I Thought of Daisy*, in which Wilson portrayed [Edna St. Vincent Millay](/wiki/Edna_St._Vincent_Millay "Edna St. Vincent Millay") as Rita Cavanaugh, Wilson and Millay remained friends throughout life.

## Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edmund_Wilson&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cold War")]

Wilson was also an outspoken critic of US [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") policies. He [refused to pay](/wiki/Tax_resistance "Tax resistance") his federal [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") from 1946 to 1955 and was later investigated by the [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service"). After a settlement, Wilson received a $25,000 fine, rather than the original $69,000 sought by the IRS. He received no jail time. In his book *[The Cold War and the Income Tax: A Protest](/wiki/The_Cold_War_and_the_Income_Tax:_A_Protest "The Cold War and the Income Tax: A Protest")* (1963), Wilson argued that as a result of competitive militarization against the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), the [civil liberties](/wiki/Civil_liberties "Civil liberties") of Americans were being paradoxically infringed under the guise of defense from Communism. For those reasons, Wilson also opposed involvement in the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War").

Selected by [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") to receive the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom"), Wilson, in absentia, was officially awarded the medal on December 6, 1963, by President [Lyndon Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_Johnson "Lyndon Johnson"). However, Wilson's view of Johnson was decidedly negative. Historian [Eric F. Goldman](/wiki/Eric_F._Goldman "Eric F. Goldman") writes in his memoir *The Tragedy of Lyndon Johnson*[[22]](#cite_note-22) that when Goldman, on behalf of Johnson, invited Wilson to read from his writings at a White House Festival of the Arts in 1965, "Wilson declined with a brusqueness that I never experienced before or after in the case of an invitation in the name of the President and First Lady."

For the academic year 1964–65, he was a Fellow on the faculty in the Center for Advanced Studies at [Wesleyan University](/wiki/Wesleyan_University "Wesleyan University").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## "Edmund Wilson regrets..."

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edmund_Wilson&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: \"Edmund Wilson regrets...\"")]

Throughout his career, Wilson often answered fan mail and outside requests for his time with this form postcard:

> Edmund Wilson regrets that it is impossible for him to: Read manuscripts, write articles or books to order, write forewords or introductions, make statements for publicity purposes, do any kind of editorial work, judge literary contests, give interviews, conduct educational courses, deliver lectures, give talks or make speeches, broadcast or appear on television, take part in writers' congresses, answer questionnaires, contribute to or take part in symposiums or 'panels' of any kind, contribute manuscripts for sales, donate copies of his books to libraries, autograph books for strangers, allow his name to be used on letterheads, supply personal information about himself, supply photographs of himself, supply opinions on literary or other subjects.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Dabney, Da Capo Press, 1997 [LCCN](/wiki/LCCN_(identifier) "LCCN (identifier)") [97-22105](https://www.loc.gov/item/97022105) [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0306808099](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0306808099 "Special:BookSources/0306808099")
